findings.title: Architectural Bottleneck: jsonable_encoder (architectural hub)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'jsonable_encoder' is called by 38 functions and has complexity 35. Changes here are high-risk.

Betweenness centrality: 0.1640 - this function sits on many execution paths between other functions.

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 35 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=4, p95=8, mean=2.6, n=1060
findings.suggested_fix: This is a critical hub. Ensure comprehensive test coverage, add defensive error handling, and consider circuit breaker pattern.
findings.why_it_matters: Bottlenecks are single points of failure that amplify bugs. High fan-in with high complexity means many callers depend on risky code.

findings.title: XSS: innerHTML assignment
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 4 co-located issues] **Direct innerHTML assignment can lead to XSS vulnerabilities** **Location**: docs/en/docs/js/termynal.js:226 **Code snippet**: ``` div.innerHTML = `${line.value || ''}`; ``` Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious scripts that: - Steal user session cookies - Capture keystrokes and credentials - Redirect users to malicious sites - Deface the application This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Test code in production: 1 issue
findings.description:
Test utilities and patterns found in what appears to be production code.

**Found:**
• Mock/stub objects (line 28)
⚠️ This file is imported by production code!
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:

1. **Move to test files:** If this is test code, move it to a test directory
2. **Use TYPE_CHECKING:** For type hints only, wrap imports in `if TYPE_CHECKING:`
3. **Environment check:** If needed at runtime, guard with environment variable

```python
# Instead of:
from unittest.mock import Mock

# Use:
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING
if TYPE_CHECKING:
from unittest.mock import Mock
```
findings.why_it_matters: This test code is imported by production code. Test dependencies add bloat, security risks, and can cause unexpected behavior in production.

findings.title: Structural bridge: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/fastapi/docs/en/docs/js/custom.js::loadVisibleTermynals:101` separates components of [5052, 10]
findings.description: `/tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/fastapi/docs/en/docs/js/custom.js::loadVisibleTermynals:101` is a structural bridge. Removing it would split the graph into components of sizes [5052, 10]. All communication between these components currently flows through this single node.
findings.suggested_fix: Reduce coupling through this node by introducing alternative call paths, extracting shared interfaces, or splitting the bridge function into independently accessible units.
findings.why_it_matters: A structural bridge is a single node whose failure disconnects entire subsystems. This creates fragile architecture where a bug, API change, or refactor in one function can cascade to all dependent components.

findings.title: Promise without .catch()
findings.description:
Promise rejection may go unhandled, causing silent failures or crashes.

**Analysis:**
📦 In function: `handleSponsorImages` (1 callers)
findings.why_it_matters: Unhandled promise rejections can crash Node.js (--unhandled-rejections=strict) or cause silent failures that are hard to debug.

findings.title: Empty catch block swallows exceptions
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] This catch block silently swallows exceptions, hiding potential bugs.
findings.suggested_fix:
Log the exception or handle it appropriately:
- Add logging to track failures
- Re-raise if recovery isn't possible
- Handle specific exception types
findings.why_it_matters: Swallowed exceptions hide bugs and make debugging extremely difficult. When something fails silently, you may not know until much later.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 4 functions in call cycle (complexity 12)
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Mutual recursion detected: 4 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/fastapi/docs/en/docs/js/termynal.js::Termynal.addRestart:157 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/fastapi/docs/en/docs/js/termynal.js::Termynal.generateRestart:129 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/fastapi/docs/en/docs/js/termynal.js::Termynal.init:74 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/fastapi/docs/en/docs/js/termynal.js::Termynal.start:96. Aggregate complexity: 12.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: String concatenation in loop
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 4 co-located issues] Variable 'attrs' concatenated 3 times inside loop (started line 240).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: Mutable default list `[]`
findings.description:
Mutable default `t = []` is shared between all calls to `read_items`.
This is a classic Python gotcha that causes surprising bugs.

**Analysis:**
📞 Called 1 times in codebase
🌐 Public function (could be called from external code)
findings.suggested_fix:
Use `None` as default and create the list inside the function:

```python
def func(t: list = None):
t = t if t is not None else []
```
findings.why_it_matters: Python evaluates default arguments once at function definition time, not at each call. If you mutate a mutable default (list, dict, set), the mutation persists across calls, causing data to leak between invocations.

findings.title: Cookie missing HttpOnly flags
findings.description:
Cookie is missing security flags that protect against common attacks.

**Analysis:**
❌ Missing: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite
📦 In function: `create_cookie`
findings.suggested_fix:
```python
response.set_cookie(
'cookie_name',
value,
httponly=True,   # Prevents JavaScript access
secure=True,     # HTTPS only
samesite='Lax'   # CSRF protection
)
```
findings.why_it_matters:
• **HttpOnly** prevents XSS attacks from stealing cookies via JavaScript
• **Secure** ensures cookies are only sent over HTTPS
• **SameSite** prevents CSRF attacks by controlling cross-site requests

findings.title: JWT using symmetric algorithm (HS256)
findings.description: HS256 uses a shared secret. If the secret is weak or leaked, attackers can forge tokens. Consider RS256/ES256.
findings.suggested_fix:
Consider using asymmetric algorithms:

```python
# RS256 (RSA) or ES256 (ECDSA) recommended
jwt.encode(payload, private_key, algorithm='RS256')

# If using HS256, ensure secret is:
# - At least 256 bits (32 bytes)
# - Cryptographically random
# - Never hardcoded
```
findings.why_it_matters: JWT vulnerabilities can allow attackers to forge authentication tokens, impersonate users, escalate privileges, or bypass authorization entirely.

findings.description:
Variable 'message' concatenated 4 times inside loop (started line 125).

**Performance:** O(n²) time complexity. Each concatenation creates a new string and copies all previous characters.

For 1000 iterations, this copies ~500,000 characters instead of 1000.
findings.suggested_fix:
Use list and join:
```python
parts = []
for item in items:
parts.append(str(item))
result = ''.join(parts)
```
Or use a list comprehension:
```python
result = ''.join(str(item) for item in items)
```
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) time complexity due to immutable string copying.

findings.title: Mutable default list `[]`
findings.description:
Mutable default `t = []` is shared between all calls to `get_query_list_default`.
This is a classic Python gotcha that causes surprising bugs.

**Analysis:**
📞 Called 1 times in codebase
⚠️ Function modifies `t` - bug will definitely manifest!
🌐 Public function (could be called from external code)
findings.suggested_fix:
Use `None` as default and create the list inside the function:

```python
def func(t: list = None):
t = t if t is not None else []
```
findings.why_it_matters: Python evaluates default arguments once at function definition time, not at each call. If you mutate a mutable default (list, dict, set), the mutation persists across calls, causing data to leak between invocations.

findings.title: Broad exception catch
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Catching generic Exception hides bugs and makes debugging difficult.

**Analysis:**
📞 Try block calls: URL, links, hasAttribute, setAttribute
findings.suggested_fix: Catch specific exceptions instead of generic Exception.
findings.why_it_matters: Broad exception catches mask unexpected errors like TypeErrors or AttributeErrors that indicate bugs in your code.

findings.title: Loose equality (==) used
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] == performs type coercion which can cause subtle bugs.

**Analysis:**
📦 In function: `start` (2 callers)
findings.why_it_matters:
Type coercion in == can cause unexpected behavior:
• '1' == 1 is true
• '' == false is true
• [] == false is true
Use === to compare both value AND type.

findings.title: Loose equality (==) used
findings.description:

findings.title: Single-yield generator: `get_db`
findings.description:
Generator `get_db` only yields once and not in a loop. Consider using a simple function with return instead.

**Why it matters:** Single-yield generators add complexity without the lazy evaluation benefits.
findings.suggested_fix:
Convert to a simple function:

```python
# Instead of:
def get_db(...):
yield some_value

# Use:
def get_db(...):
return some_value
```
findings.why_it_matters: Single-yield generators require callers to use next() or iterate, adding complexity without benefits.

findings.title: Excessive nesting: 7 levels
findings.description:
7 levels of nesting (threshold: 6).

Threshold: 5 (adaptive) | Actual: 7 | Default would be: 5 | Profile: p90=3, p95=4, mean=2.0, n=1060
findings.suggested_fix: Extract nested logic into functions or use early returns.
findings.why_it_matters: Deep nesting makes code hard to read and maintain. Each level increases cognitive load exponentially.

findings.title: Insecure deserialization: Deserialization
findings.description:
Deserializing user-controlled data with Deserialization can lead to Remote Code Execution.

**Analysis:**
🔧 Method: Deserialization
⚠️ Risk: May be unsafe
📦 In function: `create_item` (1 callers)
✅ Validation/schema check found nearby
findings.suggested_fix: Validate schema before deserializing user input.
findings.why_it_matters:
Insecure deserialization can allow attackers to:
• Execute arbitrary code on the server
• Bypass authentication
• Perform denial of service
• Access sensitive data

findings.title: Generator always list()-wrapped: `iter_en_paths_to_translate`
findings.description:
Generator `iter_en_paths_to_translate` is always wrapped in `list()`, defeating lazy evaluation.

**Analysis:** No callers consume this generator lazily.
findings.suggested_fix:
Consider returning a list directly:

```python
# Instead of:
def iter_en_paths_to_translate(...):
for item in items:
yield transform(item)

# result = list(iter_en_paths_to_translate(...))  # Always converted

# Use:
def iter_en_paths_to_translate(...):
return [transform(item) for item in items]
```
findings.why_it_matters: Generators wrapped in list() lose lazy evaluation benefits and add unnecessary overhead.
